tooltips.wastePct: Percentage of material lost during production. Typically between 2% and 10%.
tooltips.laborCost: How much your hour of work is worth. Don't give it away for free.

tooltips.desiredMarginPct: Profit percentage you want to maintain on the sale price.
tooltips.platformFeePct: Commission charged by the platform where you sell (Etsy, Amazon, etc.).

recommendations.lowMargin: Your margin is below the recommended minimum (20%). Review your costs or adjust the price.
recommendations.highWaste: Waste is significantly affecting your profitability. Consider optimizing your process.
recommendations.highPackaging: Packaging represents a high percentage of total cost. Evaluate alternatives.
recommendations.noLabor: Your labor cost is not being included correctly. Add your hourly rate.
recommendations.bulkSaving: You could improve your margin by buying materials in bulk.
recommendations.healthyMargin: Your margin is healthy. This product is ready to sell.
recommendations.notMarketplace: This product looks profitable for direct sales, but not for marketplace sales.
recommendations.setRecommend: This product may perform better as part of a set.
recommendations.minWholesale: The suggested wholesale price should not go below {price}.
recommendations.reviewBeforeSell: This product needs review before selling.

candles.title: Candle Calculator
candles.subtitle: Precisely calculate formulas for artisan candles

fragrance.title: Fragrance
fragrance.warning: Maximum recommended fragrance load is {max}% for {waxType}. Exceeding this may cause bleeding or burning issues.
fragrance.tooltipLoad: Fragrance percentage relative to total wax weight. Typically between 6% and 12%.

colorant.title: Colorant
colorant.tooltipAmount: Use approximately 0.5g–1g per 450g of wax for solid colorants.

additives.title: Additives
additives.stearicHint: Hardens wax, improves opacity and mold release. Essential for mold wax.
additives.stearicPctHint: Recommended range: 5–15%. Over 20% makes candles brittle.
additives.stearicWarnNone: Mold wax requires stearin to hold its shape. Add at least 5%.
additives.stearicWarnLow: Under 5% may not provide enough hardness for a free-standing candle.
additives.stearicWarnHigh: Over 20% may make the candle too brittle and prone to cracking.
additives.moldWaxInfo: Mold wax — High-melting-point paraffin (58–68°C) for pillar, figure, and free-standing candles. ALWAYS requires a second pour.
additives.vybarHint: Ideal range: 0.5–1% of wax weight. Over 1.5% reduces scent throw.
additives.vybarWarnHigh: Vybar above 1.5% can significantly reduce fragrance throw.
additives.vybar103Desc: For molded paraffin candles. Hardens, reduces mottling, improves surface.
additives.vybar260Desc: For soft wax blends (soy, coconut). Better fragrance retention.
additives.uvHint: Prevents yellowing of white candles. Use: 0.1–0.5% of wax weight.

warnings.highFragrance: Fragrance load is too high. May cause burning or bleeding issues.
warnings.highWaste: Waste is too high. Review your production process.

resin.title: Epoxy Resin Calculator
resin.subtitle: Calculate mix, pigments, and costs for resin projects
resin.costPerKgA: Cost per kg of Part A
resin.costPerKgB: Cost per kg of Part B

tooltips.mixRatio: The ratio indicates how much Part A per Part B. For example 2:1 means 2 parts A for every 1 part B.
tooltips.mixLoss: Percentage of resin lost during mixing and application. Typically 3–8%.
tooltips.density: Typical epoxy resin density: 1.1 g/ml. May vary by manufacturer.

soap.title: Artisan Soap Calculator
soap.subtitle: Glycerin, lye soap, solid shampoo, salt bar, milk soap — exact proportions for every method

types.milkSoap: Milk Soap (goat, coconut or oat milk)

saponification.title: Saponification (cold/hot process)
saponification.safetyWarning: ⚠️ SAFETY: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and potassium hydroxide (KOH, for liquid soap) are highly corrosive. Always wear gloves and goggles, work in a ventilated area. ALWAYS add lye to water (or to cold milk), NEVER the reverse. Do not breathe the fumes.
saponification.tooltipSuperfat: Superfat (2–8%) leaves free oils in the soap for skin softness. Higher superfat = softer soap but less cleansing.
saponification.tooltipLye: Automatically calculated using the saponification index of each oil.

soap.label: Label

concrete.title: Decorative Concrete Calculator
concrete.subtitle: Mix, pieces, and costing for artisan concrete products

mix.title: Mix composition
mix.tooltipWater: The ideal water/cement ratio is 0.4–0.5. More water weakens the mix.

warnings.tooMuchWater: Water amount is too high. This may weaken the mix and cause cracking.
warnings.tooLittleWater: Too little water may make the mix difficult to work with and affect curing.

plaster.title: Plaster & Gypsum Calculator
plaster.subtitle: Precise mix calculation, costs, and timing for plaster products

mix.title: Plaster mix
mix.tooltipRatio: Typical ratio is 0.6–0.8 parts water per 1 part plaster. Gypsum: 0.5–0.7.

comparison.title: Plaster comparison
comparison.qualityNote: Premium plaster offers better whiteness, strength, and surface detail.

compare.title: Scenario Comparator
compare.subtitle: Compare formulas, materials, prices, and strategies side by side

learn.title: Learn to Price
learn.subtitle: Mini guides for DIY entrepreneurs who want to professionalize their business

grossMargin.title: What gross margin means
grossMargin.summary: Gross margin measures how much you earn before subtracting operating expenses.
grossMargin.content: Gross margin is the difference between your selling price and the direct cost of production. It is expressed as a percentage. A 50% gross margin means that for every $100 sold, $50 covers materials and $50 remains to cover other expenses and generate profit.

netMargin.title: What net margin means
netMargin.summary: Net margin is what you actually earn after all expenses.
netMargin.content: Net margin includes all costs: materials, labor, packaging, commissions, taxes, shipping, and operating expenses. It is the most honest indicator of your business's real profitability.

waste.title: How to calculate waste
waste.summary: Waste is the material lost during production.
waste.content: Waste includes what spills, what doesn't fit in the mold, material that hardens before use, or products that fail quality checks. If you make 100 candles but 5 aren't perfect, your waste rate is 5%. Always include it in your costs.

labor.title: How to include labor
labor.summary: Your time has value. Don't give it away for free.
labor.content: Define how much your hour of work is worth. Multiply that value by the hours you dedicate to each product. Include preparation, production, finishing, and packaging. If you don't include labor, you're not seeing the real cost.

wholesale.title: How to calculate wholesale pricing
wholesale.summary: Wholesale price must cover costs and maintain a minimum margin.
wholesale.content: Wholesale price is typically double your production cost. But if your costs include labor and all expenses, the multiplier may be different. Use DIY Calc Pro to simulate it with your real numbers.

profitability.title: How to know if a product is profitable
profitability.summary: A product is profitable when it generates margin after ALL costs.
profitability.content: A profitable product covers: materials, labor, packaging, commissions, shipping, waste, and still leaves a positive net margin. If any of these variables are missing, your profitability calculation is incomplete.

multiplier.title: Why you should not price only by multiplying by 2 or 3
multiplier.summary: The 2x or 3x rule ignores critical variables that affect your profitability.
multiplier.content: Multiplying material cost by 2 or 3 is the most common and most dangerous method. It omits labor, waste, packaging, commissions, and time. In many cases, a product that 'looks' profitable this way generates losses when all variables are included.

sets.title: How to price sets
sets.summary: A set must cover the costs of all components plus presentation packaging.
sets.content: When creating a set, add up the cost of each individual component, add the special set packaging, the extra assembly time, and a margin that justifies the perceived value. A well-priced set can have better margins than each piece sold separately.

custom.title: How to price custom products
custom.summary: Customization has additional value that you should charge for.
custom.content: A custom product takes more time, involves risk of error, and has less resale potential if returned. Add a customization surcharge (typically 20–30% over the base price) and include client consultation time.

items.q: How do I activate my license after purchasing?
items.a: After purchase you'll receive an email with your activation code (format XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX). Sign in to the app, go to the Activate license section, enter the code and press Activate. Access is granted immediately.

items.q: I purchased but didn't receive my activation code
items.a: Check your spam or junk folder. If it's not there within 15 minutes, email us at servicioalcliente@seitonhome.com with your purchase confirmation and we'll send the code manually.

items.q: Can I use the app on multiple devices?
items.a: Yes. With your account you can sign in from any computer, tablet, or phone with internet access. The license is per user, not per device.

items.q: Does the app work without an internet connection?
items.a: No. DIY Calc Pro requires internet to load and save your calculations, materials, and formulas. Data is stored in the cloud so it's available on any device.

items.q: How do I export my calculations to PDF?
items.a: In each calculator, once you get results, you'll see the Export PDF button. It generates a professional cost sheet with all the calculation data, ready to print or share.
No. It's a one-time payment that includes all future updates at no additional cost.

items.q: How do I change the app language?
items.a: At the bottom of the sidebar you'll find the language selector. You can switch between Spanish and English at any time and the change applies to the entire app immediately.

items.q: Does the app sell lye, wax, or resin?
items.a: No. DIY Calc Pro is a calculation tool — it tells you how much of each material you need. You source the materials from your usual local supplier.
